author: @solarhope
text: As the material is anisotropic, do you see a difference in resistance if you take 2 square readings at 90 degree to each other, or is the effect to small to register on an AVO. Great work btw, always enjoy vids and comments.- Martin.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes - by a factor of 3

author: @picnet
text: If a drop of salt water generates electricity, I wonder if its reciprocal? In other words the ability to move the droplet of salt water by applying current between two or multiple points on the sheet? I don't know if an aniline dye is the same as aniline salt but there could be "potential" in display technology - if you excuse the pun :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think the effect arises from the difference between ion inertia and fast electron transport within the graphene sheet - so the effect would be reciprocal but the energy required to move the ionic mass would be pretty large - or at least that would be my guess - if you are thinking of rotational effects then isn't this exactly what liquid crystals do? in a semi hydrated form large sheet graphene oxide platelets have liquid crystal properties.

author: @StefanHartmann-hartiberlin
text:
By the way, I am pretty sure you only get 900 Millivolts Voltage, cause your lower lead croco clip is not isolated and is in the water. If you would isolate the contact point fully, so no water would come into contact with BOTH contact points of the graphite painted PET sheet, your voltage would only be in the lower 1 to 30 millivolts range probably... Have had these problems myself when trying out new battery concepts...
Would be great, if you could confirm this. Many thanks.
Regards, Stefan.

author: @StefanHartmann-hartiberlin
text:
Robert, how much voltage was that ? It is hard to see, please always tell the values with your voice. Was it 0.8 Volts ?
Was your connection lead clip also in the water ? If yes, this is just a dissimular metal galvanic cell you really have to isolate all your contact electrode materials from the water, otherwise you get dissimilar metal effects and thus an galvanic cell. so just coat the contacts with plastic spray or hot glue, so it does not get any contact with the water at all...also any copper contact strip needs to be isolated from the water..
Regards. Stefan.

author: @TheBossGrand
text: Do you think this material could be used to create an effective earth battery?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it can certainly be used to create effective batteries but I am afraid I know very little about earth batteries

replies.author: @TheBossGrand
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith There hasn't been much innovation in earth batteries since the days of the telegraph. They use two rods of different materials usual iron and copper to generate voltage. The problem with this though is that the process of generating electricity corrodes the rods thus a long lasting earth battery isn't viable. I wonder if the same effect would occur with the material you made.

author: @directeeccks
text:
Thanks for posting this and this was the exact the kind of testing I wanted to do with the ink.  I have done a lot of testing with thermal electrics due to the nature of benefits of non moving parts and so forth.  I have even went as far as binding copper and iron  together in a V shape in series to hand make one myself.  The results where okay for my testing but not what I am shooting for. The cost of commercial ones make it not very cost effective per watt with the included thermal sources input cost.  This is pretty exciting I must say cause that is just one sheet.  Thermal electrics can increase efficiency by stacking them on top of one another from one thermal input. 

If your going to do some more testing try seeing if you can create a current with just your hand and the ambient temp with one sheet. It will only increase with a little bit of engineering and stacking them.   I am crossing my fingers for ambient temp and your hand is enough to generate some numbers. Since you have plastic as your substrate  this may will give you more actuate numbers cause no warping or expanding is happening or at least a good control. Then you can get some control numbers that are good enough to calculate efficiency.  If you can get anything 5 percent or above you will basically beat most commercial ones hand over fist on cost. 

I honestly would be up for 5 days strait testing this.   The amount of applications due the nature of the material and along with its cost.  So many ideas I have had and now I have probably a hundred more.  I am not going to get any sleep tonight XD thanks hahahah.  So many ideas........  The surface area would give you good shot at using evaporation to create a difference without using heat as your input.  Also the surface area could lend it self to a good large heat sink to create a difference.   Honest I could go on for days of practical experiments to test out this application.  So I am going to stop here.

I think you should get maybe a google live hangout or skype call going at some point in time.  It will really be crucial after you ship out the inks.  Adafruit does a show called "show and tell and ask a engineer"  and a lot of people participate.  Google has even recognize the amount of people that show up on them. Since we are all scattered all across the world and text can only go so far.  LOL I nerded out, I apologize much love and cheers!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: dude - that is one long list lol - where are you based?

replies.author: @directeeccks
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith
I am based in the states. Lol My bad I got super excited..Your enthusiasm rubs off. XD Don't mind me just there is just so many gaps a thermal electric "could" fill that other power harvesting techniques leave empty. I have a probable invested way to much time on it but I see tons of potential.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
directeeccks
that is a shame - i was going to invite you down to the lab for a week - ah well some other time maybe
